---
title: "Plotting Basics with `MatPlotlib` and `Seaborn` - Answer Key"
author:
  - Noor Sohail
  - Will Gammerdinger
date: "2026-03-15"
license: "CC-BY-4.0"
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r}
#| label: load_libraries_data
#| echo: false
# Load libraries and data
# Interfacing with R quarto and python futzing
library(reticulate)
use_condaenv("/opt/anaconda3/envs/intro_python", required = TRUE)
```

```{python}
#| label: load_libraries_data_py
#| echo: false
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd 

# Load the new metadata data frame that we created in the previous lesson
new_metadata = pd.read_csv("data/new_metadata.csv", index_col=0)  
```

# Exercise 1

1. The current axis label text defaults to what we gave as input to `sns.scatterplot` (i.e the column headers). We can change this by **adding additional layers** called `plt.xlabel()` and `plt.ylabel()` for the x- and y-axis, respectively. Add these layers to the current plot such that the x-axis is labeled "Age (days)" and the y-axis is labeled "Mean expression".

```{python}
#| label: exercise_1
# Set the theme to "whitegrid"
sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Initialize a plot with a specific size
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))

# Add a scatterplot layer to the plot, coloring points by genotype
sns.scatterplot(data = new_metadata,
                x="age_in_days", 
                y="mean_expression",
                hue="genotype",
                style="celltype",
                s=50)

# Change the size of the axis label
plt.xlabel(xlabel = "Age in Days",
           fontsize=20)

# Add y-axis label
plt.ylabel(ylabel = "Mean Expression",
           fontsize=20)
``` 

2. Use the `plt.title()` layer to add a plot title of your choice. 

```{python}
#| label: exercise_2
# Set the theme to "whitegrid"
sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Initialize a plot with a specific size
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))

# Add a scatterplot layer to the plot, coloring points by genotype
sns.scatterplot(data = new_metadata,
                x="age_in_days", 
                y="mean_expression",
                hue="genotype",
                style="celltype",
                s=50)

# Change the size of the axis label
plt.xlabel(xlabel = "Age in Days",
           fontsize=20)

# Add y-axis label
plt.ylabel(ylabel = "Mean Expression",
           fontsize=20)

# Add title label to plot
plt.title(label = "Mean expression vs Age (Days)")
``` 

3. When you add the arguments `loc="right"` to the `plt.title()` function, what does it change?

```{python}
#| label: exercise_3
# Set the theme to "whitegrid"
sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Initialize a plot with a specific size
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))

# Add a scatterplot layer to the plot, coloring points by genotype
sns.scatterplot(data = new_metadata,
                x="age_in_days", 
                y="mean_expression",
                hue="genotype",
                style="celltype",
                s=50)

# Change the size of the axis label
plt.xlabel(xlabel = "Age in Days",
           fontsize=20)

# Add y-axis label
plt.ylabel(ylabel = "Mean Expression",
           fontsize=20)

# Add title label to plot
plt.title(label = "Mean expression vs Age (Days)",
          loc="right")
``` 

4. Try adding the layer `plt.legend(loc="center right")` to the end of your code. What does this do? How many layers can be added to a plot, in your estimation?


```{python}
#| label: exercise_4
# Set the theme to "whitegrid"
sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# Initialize a plot with a specific size
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))

# Add a scatterplot layer to the plot, coloring points by genotype
sns.scatterplot(data = new_metadata,
                x="age_in_days", 
                y="mean_expression",
                hue="genotype",
                style="celltype",
                s=50)

# Change the size of the axis label
plt.xlabel(xlabel = "Age in Days",
           fontsize=20)

# Add y-axis label
plt.ylabel(ylabel = "Mean Expression",
           fontsize=20)

# Add title label to plot
plt.title(label = "Mean expression vs Age (Days)")

# Change legend location
plt.legend(loc="center right")
``` 

`plt.legend` changes the location of the legend in the plot. We can add as many layers as we would like.
